front: What does the "knock and talk" doctrine actually rest on — and is it a standalone warrant exception?
back: It is NOT a standalone warrant exception. It rests on the same implied license that lets any visitor approach a home's front door, knock, wait briefly, and (absent a refusal) leave.

front: In knock-and-talk doctrine, what is a lawful approach vs. a lawful search — and what does a knock and talk authorize by itself?
back: A knock and talk is a lawful approach, not a lawful search. By itself it authorizes no search; any entry or search still needs valid consent or an independent warrant exception.

front: When officers use the knock-and-talk to ask for consent to search, what body of law governs what they obtain?
back: Ordinary consent-search law governs. The knock and talk just gets officers lawfully to the door; voluntariness of any consent is judged under standard consent doctrine.

front: The knock-and-talk approach becomes a Fourth Amendment search the moment officers exceed the implied license in {{c1::scope}}, {{c2::time}}, or {{c3::manner}}.
back: Scope, time, or manner. Examples: straying off the normal route, lingering after being told to go, or bringing investigative tools onto the curtilage.

front: Under Florida v. Jardines, what two dimensions limit the implied license to approach a home?
back: Physical area (the customary path to the front door) and purpose (a brief, ordinary visitor's errand).

front: What did the Supreme Court hold in Florida v. Jardines (2013)?
back: Walking a drug dog onto the home's front porch to investigate exceeded the implied license to approach and knock — a trespassory search of the curtilage.

front: Which case held that bringing a drug-detection dog onto a home's front porch to investigate was a trespassory search exceeding the implied license?
back: Florida v. Jardines, 569 U.S. 1 (2013).

front: On what Fourth Amendment theory did Jardines find a search — privacy or property?
back: The Jones property-trespass theory: officers physically intruded on the curtilage beyond what the implied license authorized, so it was a search without needing a privacy analysis.

front: Under Kentucky v. King (2011), may police lawfully knock and announce their presence at a door?
back: Yes. Police may lawfully knock and announce their presence; doing so does not create an unusable (manufactured) exigency.

front: Under Kentucky v. King, when is a police-created exigency unusable to justify a warrantless entry?
back: Only when police create the exigency by engaging or threatening to engage in conduct that itself violates the Fourth Amendment.

front: Quote the Kentucky v. King formulation of when the exigent-circumstances rule applies despite police involvement.
back: "The exigent circumstances rule applies when the police do not create the exigency by engaging or threatening to engage in conduct that violates the Fourth Amendment."

front: Which case establishes that merely knocking and announcing is fine, and that a police-created exigency is unusable only when the police's conduct itself violates the Fourth Amendment?
back: Kentucky v. King, 563 U.S. 452 (2011).

front: Under Florida v. Bostick, what is the test for whether an encounter stays consensual?
back: Whether a reasonable person would feel free to decline the officers' requests or otherwise terminate the encounter.

front: Which case supplies the "free to decline the requests or terminate the encounter" standard for consensual encounters?
back: Florida v. Bostick, 501 U.S. 429 (1991).

front: What did United States v. Drayton (2002) hold about advising a person of the right to refuse consent?
back: Consent can be voluntary even though officers never advised the person of the right to refuse; there is no per se warning requirement.

front: Which case holds there is no per se requirement to warn a person of the right to refuse before consent can be voluntary?
back: United States v. Drayton, 536 U.S. 194 (2002).

front: What is the combined Bostick/Drayton rule for keeping a knock-and-talk consensual?
back: The encounter stays consensual if a reasonable person would feel free to decline the requests or terminate the encounter (Bostick), and officers need not advise the resident of the right to refuse (Drayton).

front: What is the precedential weight of French v. Merrill (1st Cir. 2021) on knock-and-talk limits, and how should it be labeled?
back: Persuasive only — a First Circuit marker, not a nationwide rule. Lower courts vary on repeated knocks, back-door approaches, and pre-dawn visits.

front: In French v. Merrill (1st Cir. 2021), what officer conduct exceeded the implied license to enter the curtilage?
back: Repeated pre-dawn visits, knocking on a bedroom window, and peering in through a window covering with a flashlight — intrusive, contentious conduct no reasonable visitor would understand as authorized.

front: How did the First Circuit in French v. Merrill describe the officers' conduct relative to the curtilage license?
back: Their conduct "plainly exceeded the scope of the implied license to enter the curtilage of French's home."
cite: French v. Merrill, 15 F.4th 116 (1st Cir. 2021) (slip op. at 43)

front: How did French v. Merrill tie the officers' conduct to Jardines for qualified-immunity purposes?
back: The court held the officers, like those in Jardines, physically intruded on the property without license in a way no reasonable visitor could understand as authorized — making the violation clearly established and defeating qualified immunity.
cite: French v. Merrill, 15 F.4th 116 (1st Cir. 2021) (slip op. at 40)

front: Distinguish: what is within the implied license at the front door vs. what breaks it (per Jardines)?
back: Within: walking the customary front-door path, knocking, and waiting briefly for an ordinary errand. Breaks it: deploying a drug dog on the porch, cutting to a back door or yard, or lingering for an investigative purpose.

front: Distinguish lawful knocking from manufacturing exigency under Kentucky v. King.
back: Lawfully knocking and announcing is permitted and does not taint a resulting emergency. Banging while threatening to break in — i.e., threatening conduct that itself violates the Fourth Amendment — manufactures an exigency that cannot justify a warrantless entry.

front: Common pitfall: officers treat the knock and talk as its own warrant exception. Why is that wrong?
back: It is not an exception. Without consent or another independent exception, nothing inside the home becomes fair game; the doctrine only authorizes a lawful approach.

front: Common pitfall: ignoring curtilage / implied-license limits during a knock and talk. What breaks the license?
back: Cutting to a back door, lingering in the yard, or bringing a dog onto the porch — anything beyond the front-door path for a brief errand (Jardines).

front: Common pitfall: what happens when officers overstay a resident's refusal during a knock and talk?
back: Once a resident declines or asks officers to leave, continued or repeated intrusion converts the encounter into a search — as the First Circuit illustrated in French v. Merrill.

front: Common pitfall: using the knock to manufacture exigency. What conduct taints any resulting "emergency"?
back: Banging while shouting threats to break in, or otherwise threatening a Fourth Amendment violation, taints the emergency and cannot justify a warrantless entry (King).

front: Why does the knock-and-talk doctrine exist — what legal fact makes the approach lawful without a warrant?
back: Because any visitor has an implied license to approach the front door, knock, wait briefly, and leave; officers may use that same customary license to seek a consensual conversation or ask for consent.
